channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Aftermath of Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, a photographer for The New York Times, describes the scene on the ground after the Venezuelan earthquakes.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, Leila Medina and Estelle Caswell

item.title: How Long Can a Person Survive Under Rubble?
item.description: It depends on water, air and how quickly rescuers are able to find people.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue teams walking through a building that collapsed during the earthquakes in Venezuela.

item.title: Hoping for Miracles as Venezuela Digs Out From Massive Quakes
item.description: It is known as a “doublet,” two earthquakes in quick succession, and it has brought the Latin American country to its knees.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Anatoly Kurmanaev, Julie Turkewitz, Isayen Herrera and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Looking for survivors on Thursday in the rubble of a building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, that collapsed a day earlier in an earthquake.

item.title: Tighter Canadian Borders Limit U.S. Deportees Seeking Refuge
item.description: New Canadian policies and a longstanding border agreement with the United States will make heading north a tough route for Syrians and Haitians.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Nathan Denette/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Trudeau welcoming Syrian refugees to Toronto in December 2015.

item.title: After Supreme Court TPS Ruling, What Conditions May Haitian and Syrian Deportees Face?
item.description: The Supreme Court ruled that the Trump administration could end humanitarian protections for many Haitians and Syrians in the United States, clearing the way for their potential deportation.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Haitian physician who fled his homeland in 2021, at his home in Springfield, Ohio, in January. About 350,000 Haitians had received protection from deportation in the United States.

item.title: Devastating Earthquakes Will Test Venezuela’s Newfound Alliance With U.S.
item.description: President Trump has boasted of the success of an armed intervention in Venezuela. A natural disaster raises pressure to match rhetoric with action.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A person searches the ruins of an apartment building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: On the ground and online, Venezuelans desperately search for missing relatives.
item.description: Friends and family of those in the earthquake zone were turning to social media and heading to affected neighborhoods as they awaited an official rescue response.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Sheyla Urdaneta and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Searching for survivors in the rubble in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, La Guaira Residents Search for Loved Ones in the Rubble
item.description: A missing child. A boyfriend in the rubble. Venezuelans on Thursday were still trying to understand the scope of the devastation in this hard-hit city.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera, Julie Turkewitz and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: In Catia La Mar, a port city in La Guaira State in Venezuela, residents said they had seen few rescue workers at the scene after the earthquakes on Wednesday night, leaving civilians to look for survivors.

item.title: How to help victims of the Venezuela earthquakes.
item.description: Here’s how to contribute to organizations that are aiding the rescue and recovery efforts in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue efforts in Caracas, Venezuela, on Wednesday after two large earthquakes struck the country.

item.title: Why the Venezuelan Earthquakes Happened, and What to Expect Next
item.description: Twin earthquakes like those that ripped through the region are unusual but not unheard of. Scientists are already gathering data needed for a more detailed picture.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: Leonardo Fernandez Viloria/Reuters
item.media:description: Emergency workers at the site of a collapsed building in Caracas on Thursday.

item.title: Supreme Court Expands Trump’s Power Over Immigration With TPS and Southern Border Decisions
item.description: The court handed President Trump victories in his push to rescind deportation protections for hundreds of thousands of people and turn away migrants at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s 6-to-3 decision in two cases with broad implications divided along ideological lines, with the liberal justices dissenting.

item.title: Is Canada About to Enter the Eurovision Song Contest?
item.description: As Canada’s government looks more toward Europe and less to the United States, its national broadcaster has become a full member of the group that organizes the glitzy singing event.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Eurovision Song Contest final in Vienna in May.

item.title: Driver Plows Into Crowd of World Cup Fans in Mexico, Authorities Say
item.description: Videos show a car repeatedly accelerating into a crowd in Cabo San Lucas after Mexico won a World Cup game. The authorities said at least 17 people were injured and the driver was arrested.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and James McManagan
item.media:credit: Alfredo Estrella/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A file photo shows tourists in Los Cabos, an area of Baja California Sur, Mexico, which includes the city of Cabo San Lucas.

item.title: Earthquakes Are Latest Challenge for Venezuelan Leader
item.description: President Delcy Rodríguez, in power only for a few months, has been trying to balance the demands of President Trump and her own domestic political needs.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Federico Parra/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Delcy Rodríguez became leader after the U.S. military deposed and captured her predecessor, Nicolás Maduro.

item.title: Venezuela Earthquakes Hammer Infrastructure, Making Aid Efforts Harder
item.description: The power grid, water supply and health system were already under strain from years of neglect and underinvestment.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rubble of a residential building that collapsed during an earthquake in Caracas, on Wednesday.

item.title: Two Earthquakes Hit Venezuela: What to Know About Death Toll, Damage and Rescue Response
item.description: Two major quakes struck back to back on Wednesday night in the country’s populated northern areas. The second was the strongest to hit Venezuela in over a century.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency workers tried to rescue people trapped in the rubble of a residential building in Caracas, Venezuela, on Wednesday.

item.title: La Guaira, Venezuelan Port Town, Is Hit Hard by Earthquakes
item.description: Deadly mudslides in 1999 dealt lasting blows to impoverished La Guaira. The response of the acting president, Delcy Rodríguez, may be a bellwether of her political longevity.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev

item.title: Several Strong Quakes Hit Across the World in 24 Hours
item.description: Experts said the powerful earthquakes that struck California and Japan are likely not connected to the devastating tremors in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff and Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Kyodo, via Reuters
item.media:description: A pedestrian passes a damaged wall of a building following an earthquake in Hachinohe, Aomori Prefecture, Japan, on Thursday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Anatoly Kurmanaev, Julie Turkewitz and Isayen Herrera

item.title: A Right-Wing Election Victory in Colombia, With a Little Help from Trump
item.description: The U.S. president endorsed the right-wing winner. But his influence went further.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Abelardo de la Espriella in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday during the presidential runoff election.

item.title: These Birds’ Backflips Are Fueled by Sugar
item.description: An ancient dietary change made the manakin’s flashy courtship display possible, a new study suggests.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Venezuela Residents Describe Terror as Deadly Earthquake Struck
item.description: Buildings collapsed, windows rattled and residents poured into the streets in Venezuela’s capital and nearby cities after two major earthquakes hit.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky, Tibisay Romero, Fabiola Ferrero, Julie Turkewitz, María Victoria Fermín and Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A couple cries in front of a building that collapsed during the earthquakes that struck Venezuela on Wednesday.

item.title: Venezuela’s Neighbors Offer Aid in Earthquake Response
item.description: Officials from the United States, El Salvador and the Dominican Republic said they would send rescue teams and humanitarian assistance to Caracas.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency services searching the rubble of a collapsed building on Wednesday.

item.title: 7.5-Magnitude Quake Is Largest to Hit Venezuela in More Than a Century
item.description: The U.S. Geological Survey estimated that it could be far deadlier than a 7.7-magnitude earthquake that struck the country in 1900.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Photos: Venezuela Earthquakes Cause Death and Destruction in Caracas, La Guaira and Beyond
item.description: Two powerful quakes shook the South American country, causing death and destruction.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians and emergency workers walking through the rubble of a building that collapsed during an earthquake, on Thursday in Catia La Mar, Venezuela.

item.title: Venezuela Hit by 7.1-Magnitude Earthquake
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín and Simon Romero

item.title: Cuba Announces Sweeping Overhaul of Struggling Communist Economy, But Obstacles Remain
item.description: The Cuban government announced a major economic restructuring, but experts say most changes would depend on buy-in from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Eliana Aponte for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers in the production workshop of Dforja Creations, a private furniture company in Havana, in 2024.

item.title: ICC Judges Sue Trump Administration Over Sanctions
item.description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, and two colleagues are suing the administration, saying the sanctions were a form of retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Amanda Taub
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Geert Vanden Wijngaert/Reuters
item.media:description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, along with two others, is suing President Trump and his administration, claiming the penalties exceeded his authority.

item.title: Could Brazil’s Next Big Soccer Star Be Scouted By AI?
item.description: Recruitment apps powered by artificial intelligence are gaining ground in Brazil, promising to even the playing field and find more soccer talent.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Victor Moriyama

item.title: Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s President, Struggles to Uphold Trump’s Narrative of Success
item.description: President Trump says Venezuela, under U.S. oversight, has “never made the money” it is making now. But new oil revenue isn’t helping ordinary Venezuelans, and anger seems to be mounting.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Delcy Rodríguez, the interim president of Venezuela, during a meeting with American officials in Caracas in April.

item.title: Ramiro Valdés Menéndez, Architect of Cuba’s Surveillance State, Dies at 94
item.description: Considered the country’s most powerful leader after the Castro brothers, he was the first director of the Interior Ministry, keeping a close eye on dissent.
item.dc:creator: Anthony DePalma
item.media:credit: Adalberto Roque/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Ramiro Valdés Menéndez in 2003. A veteran of the earliest days of the Cuban revolution, he was alternately in and out of favor during his long career, but he bounced back each time, stronger than ever.

item.title: Suspect and a Civilian Are Among Three Killed in Montreal Shooting
item.description: A police officer and a civilian also died in a gun battle that prompted a citywide shelter-in-place alert.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi and Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Nasuna Stuart-Ulin for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mother and daughter were reunited after the shooting in the Côte-des-Neiges neighborhood in Montreal.

item.title: How Will Colombia’s New President Fuel Trump’s Drug War?
item.description: Abelardo De La Espriella, who has vowed to destroy traffickers with military power, appears set to be the country’s next president.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo de la Espriella on Sunday night in Barranquilla, Colombia, after preliminary results showed him to be the winner in the presidential runoff.

item.title: Cuba’s Fuel Crisis Brings Schools to a Standstill
item.description: The country’s already-struggling schools are ending the academic year early because of a crippling fuel shortage caused by the U.S. oil blockade.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole González for The New York Times
item.media:description: Analeidis Arias Matos getting her son, Alejandro, ready for school last month in Santiago, Cuba.

item.title: Abelardo De La Espriella, Trump-Backed Rightist, Headed for Win in Colombia
item.description: A victory for Abelardo De La Espriella, a lawyer with no previous political experience, would be another win for the right in Latin America.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Mr. De La Espriella in Barranquilla on Sunday.

item.title: Colombia’s High-Stakes Election: What to Know About the Candidates, Issues and Expected Results
item.description: Colombia’s election on Sunday pits a right-wing candidate endorsed by President Trump against a candidate promising continuity with the country’s departing leftist president.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky and Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Joaquin Sarmiento Juan Barreto/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Iván Cepeda, left, and Abelardo De La Espriella will face off on Sunday in a presidential runoff election in Colombia.

item.title: Huge Blaze Ravages Dominican Republic Beach Resort
item.description: The fire on Friday forced the emergency evacuation of nearly 1,700 guests. Authorities said at least one person, an Italian tourist, was killed.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: via Reuters
item.media:description: A screen shot showing the Viva Wyndham Dominicus Beach Hotel on fire in Bayahibe, Dominican Republic, on Friday.

item.title: Canada’s Postcard Moment at the World Cup Is More Than the Team’s Victory
item.description: A devastating injury to midfielder Ismaël Koné showed the squad’s spirit.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Agustin Marcarian/Reuters
item.media:description: Nathan Saliba, a player with Canada’s team who replaced the midfielder Ismaël Koné after his injury, held up a team jersey with his teammate’s surname and number, after scoring a goal.

item.title: A Court Case Reveals Some of Tim Hortons’ Secrets
item.description: The doughnut and coffee chain has an elaborate system for raising prices and making sure loyal customers pay for their rewards.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Ian Austen/The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of Tim Hortons restaurant owners in Quebec, which does not include the owners of this store in Gatineau, claim that the company has cheated them out of millions of dollars.

item.title: Abelardo De La Espriella, Right-Wing Outsider, Could Be Colombia’s Next President
item.description: President Trump endorsed Abelardo De La Espriella, the conservative presidential candidate and apparent winner of Sunday’s election who has strong U.S. ties.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Patricia Mazzei and Jorge Valencia
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: A campaign rally last month in Medellín, Colombia, for Abelardo De La Espriella, the right-wing presidential candidate.
